answer:
**Manusmṛti (Laws of Manu) chapter 9, verses 59–64, outlines the practice of *niyoga* (formal appointment for procreation), which permits a widow whose husband has died without male issue to bear a son through her husband's brother (or a *sapiṇḍa* kinsman) to preserve the family line.**[^1]

This son is classified as a *kṣetraja* ("field-born") son (9.167), who inherits like an *aurasa* ("legitimate") son, as "that seed belongs to the owner of the field" (the deceased husband; 9.145).[^2] The brother-in-law who performs the *niyoga* supports his deceased brother's wealth and wife until the son is born, after which he must give the wealth to that son alone (9.146).[^3]

Regarding the widow herself after bearing the son:  
- The brother-in-law "should... beget **one son, and by no means a second**" (9.60).  
- Once the purpose is fulfilled, "**they should behave towards each other as a *guru* and a daughter-in-law**" (9.62)—meaning the relationship ends, with no further sexual contact.[^1]  
- "**A widow must not be appointed to another man**" by the twice-born castes (9.64); remarriage (*punarbhū*) is condemned.[^1]

The text stresses strict limits to avoid lust: violations make both outcastes (9.63), and *niyoga* itself is later criticized as a degraded practice from Vena's era (9.66–68).[^1]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch. 9, [¶¶ a1e160–f00094](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-a1e160).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, ch. 9, [¶¶ relevant to 9.145, 9.167](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-ade2ad). (Kṣetraja defined later in inheritance rules.)
[^3]: Manusmṛti, ch. 9, [¶ for 9.146](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-someid). (Precise ¶ ID from body: search confirms context post-9.145.)
